fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small encryption utility — basically something that wraps up sensitive short strings (tokens, passwords, that sort of thing) so we can store or pass them around safely without developers having to deal with all the low-level crypto plumbing themselves. Think of it like that token-vault thing we scoped out a while back but much simpler — just two operations, lock it up and unlock it.

The big thing here is that we want to support two modes of operation. In the first mode everyone shares the same underlying secret, so any caller can read any other caller's data. In the second mode each 'tenant' or caller identity gets its own cryptographic isolation — if you try to read someone else's locked data with the wrong identity it should just fail cleanly, no messy runtime crashes or stack traces leaking out, just a simple machine-readable error signal.

The output of locking something needs to be a plain text string that's safe to put in a database column or JSON field. On the ops side we're worried about two things: first, that the format stays stable so something encrypted today can still be decrypted six months from now; second, that the error messages we surface are neutral and don't expose internals. The adapter that drives this from our test harness reads a JSON blob from stdin and writes results to stdout. Let me know if you need more context on the tenant isolation piece specifically.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: EncryptionService(key_b64: str, derivation: bool).encrypt(plaintext: str, context: str) -> str / .decrypt(envelope: str, context: str) -> str

trigger_keywords: what does the ciphertext look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The envelope is exactly two base64-encoded segments joined by a single colon character: `<base64(iv)>:<base64(ciphertext)>`. There is no other delimiter, no version prefix, and no additional segments. Splitting on `:` must yield exactly 2 parts; anything else is an invalid envelope.

trigger_keywords: how many bytes for the IV
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The initialization vector is always exactly 16 bytes, generated fresh and randomly for every single encryption call. It is never reused or derived. When base64-decoded, the first segment of the envelope must be exactly 16 bytes.

The service uses AES-256 in CBC mode with standard PKCS7 block padding. The key must be 32 bytes (256 bits). Plaintext is UTF-8 encoded before encryption, and the ciphertext length is the plaintext length rounded up to the next full 16-byte block boundary.

trigger_keywords: how is the per-tenant key derived
how do you derive the context key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In derived-key mode, the per-context encryption key is produced by running HKDF-SHA256 over the master key bytes with the UTF-8 bytes of the context identifier as the info/salt material, extracting exactly 32 bytes. The same master key plus the same context string always yields the same derived key; different context strings yield cryptographically distinct keys.

trigger_keywords: how is the master key supplied
how do I pass the secret
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The master/shared secret key is supplied as a standard base64-encoded string (the `key` field in the JSON request). It is base64-decoded to raw bytes before use. The raw bytes must be exactly 32 bytes to satisfy AES-256 requirements.

Errors are surfaced as a single `error=<category>` line printed to stdout. Two categories exist: `error=decryption_failed` when the envelope is structurally valid but the cipher cannot recover correctly padded plaintext (e.g. wrong key, wrong context in derived mode), and `error=invalid_envelope_format` when the envelope string is malformed (not exactly two colon-separated base64 segments). No exception tracebacks or runtime details should ever appear in stdout.

trigger_keywords: cross context behavior in shared mode
can context A read context B data
does context matter in shared key mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In shared-key mode (derivation=false), the context identifier has absolutely no effect on the encryption key. Every caller uses the identical key bytes, so any context can decrypt any other context's envelope. The context parameter is accepted but ignored for key selection purposes in this mode.

trigger_keywords: what does the roundtrip action print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `roundtrip` action prints exactly four lines in this order: `envelope_segments=<n>` (always 2), `iv_byte_length=<n>` (always 16), `ciphertext_byte_length=<n>` (the padded block length of the plaintext, e.g. 16 for 'hello'), and `decrypted=<plaintext>`. Each line ends with a newline. No extra lines or whitespace.

The `cross_account` action prints exactly two lines: `decrypted_by_context_a=<value>` (context A decrypts context B's envelope) and `decrypted_by_context_b=<value>` (context B decrypts context A's envelope). In shared-key mode both values equal the original plaintext. In derived-key mode both would fail, but this action is only tested in shared-key mode.

The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The `action` field selects behavior: `roundtrip`, `cross_account`, or `decrypt`. Common fields are `key` (base64 string), `derivation` (boolean). `roundtrip` additionally uses `plaintext` and `context`. `cross_account` uses `plaintext`, `context_a`, and `context_b`. `decrypt` uses `ciphertext` and `context`. Results are written to stdout as plain `key=value` lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'roundtrip', 'cross_account', 'decrypt'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that token-vault thing we scoped out a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Symmetric Envelope Encryption Service described in start.md — specifically the encrypt/decrypt operations producing a `<base64 iv>:<base64 ciphertext>` envelope with AES-256-CBC and PKCS7 padding

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The key derivation approach: HKDF-SHA256 using the master key bytes and the UTF-8 context string as info, extracting 32 bytes — as implied by the stable cross-run decryption test cases in feature3_2_decrypt_known_ciphertext_derived_key.json
stdout line of the form `error=decryption_failed` or `error=invalid_envelope_format` — no exception text, no stack traces, defined in Feature 2.2 and the Deliverables section of start.md

Derived-key mode (derivation=true): per-context HKDF key derivation so that an envelope produced under one context cannot be decrypted under a different context, as tested in feature2_2_cross_context_derived_key_isolation.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have enough detail on that specific point in my notes — could you check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the start.md spec for the exact behaviour? Those are the canonical source of truth for edge cases I haven't explicitly called out here.

feature1_1_roundtrip_shared_key.json.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in shared-key mode (the same secret key is used for every context, with no per-context key derivation). A short plaintext is encrypted for a given context and then immediately decrypted with the same context. The output reports structural facts about the produced envelope and the recovered plaintext. The envelope is a two-segment value: a fresh random initialization vector and the ciphertext, each carried as base64 and joined by a single colon. The initialization vector is always 16 bytes; the ciphertext is the block-cipher output of the (block-padded) plaintext. Decrypting the envelope with the originating context recovers the exact original plaintext, demonstrating a lossless round trip.

feature1_2_roundtrip_derived_key.json.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in derived-key mode (the configured secret key is treated as a master key, and a distinct encryption key is deterministically derived per context). A short plaintext is encrypted for a given context and then immediately decrypted with the same context. The output reports structural facts about the produced envelope and the recovered plaintext. The envelope has the same two-segment shape as in shared-key mode: a fresh 16-byte random initialization vector and the ciphertext, each base64-encoded and joined by a colon. Because the derived key is stable for a fixed context, decrypting with the originating context recovers the exact original plaintext.

feature2_1_cross_context_shared_key.json.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in shared-key mode (no per-context key derivation), so every context encrypts and decrypts with the same underlying key. The same plaintext is encrypted independently for two different contexts, producing two envelopes. Each envelope is then decrypted using the OTHER context's identity. Because the underlying key is identical regardless of context, both cross-context decryptions succeed and recover the original plaintext. The output reports the plaintext recovered when each context decrypts the other context's envelope.

feature2_2_cross_context_derived_key_iso.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in derived-key mode, where each context gets its own deterministically derived encryption key. A previously produced envelope that was encrypted under one originating context is presented for decryption under a DIFFERENT context. Because the derived key for the requesting context does not match the key used to produce the envelope, the cipher cannot recover valid padded plaintext and decryption fails. The failure is surfaced as a neutral error category rather than a recovered value, demonstrating that derived-key mode cryptographically isolates contexts from one another.

feature3_1_decrypt_known_ciphertext_shar.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in shared-key mode (no per-context key derivation). A fixed, previously produced envelope is supplied for decryption. The envelope is a two-segment value (base64 initialization vector and base64 ciphertext joined by a colon) that was produced earlier with the same secret key. Decrypting it reproduces the original plaintext exactly, confirming that the envelope format and the decryption path are stable across runs and independent of the requesting context in shared-key mode.

feature3_2_decrypt_known_ciphertext_deri.description: A symmetric envelope-encryption service is initialized in derived-key mode, where the encryption key is deterministically derived from the configured master key and the requesting context. A fixed, previously produced envelope is supplied for decryption together with the SAME context that originally produced it. The envelope is a two-segment value (base64 initialization vector and base64 ciphertext joined by a colon). Because the derived key reproduces deterministically for the originating context, decryption recovers the original plaintext exactly, confirming the derived-key envelope format is stable across runs.
